1: A direct connection: 'O LORD, our Lord, how majestic is your name in all the earth! You have set your glory above the heavens.' Ps 8 is the psalm the NT most explicitly applies to Christ. The majestic name 'in all the earth' anticipates the name 'above every name' (Philippians 2:9-10). The glory set above the heavens is what the Son shared with the Father before the world existed (John 17:5: 'Father, glorify me in your own presence with the glory that I had with you before the world existed') and what the incarnation temporarily veiled.
2: A direct connection: Jesus quotes this verse in Matthew 21:16 in response to the Pharisees' objection to the children crying 'Hosanna!' in the temple. 'Have you never read, Out of the mouth of infants and nursing babies you have prepared praise?' The children's Hosannas in the temple are the fulfillment of Psalms 8:2 — the praise of the weak silences the enemy. Christ's claim that the children's praise fulfills Ps 8 means he identifies himself as the one whose name elicits this praise.
3: A revelation of God: 'when I look at your heavens, the work of your fingers, the moon and the stars, which you have set in place' — the created order as the work of divine fingers. The creator who set the stars in place (Psalms 147:4: 'he determines the number of the stars; he gives to all of them their names') became flesh in the incarnation. John 1:3: 'all things were made through him, and without him was not any thing made that was made.' The creator entered his creation as the creature the next verses describe.
4: A direct connection: Hebrews 2:6-9 quotes this verse as applying to Christ: 'it has been testified somewhere, What is man, that you are mindful of him, or the son of man, that you care for him?' The 'son of man' (ben adam) of Psalms 8:4 is applied in Hebrews to Jesus, who 'for a little while was made lower than the angels' (= the incarnation and humiliation of Psalms 8:5a), then 'crowned with glory and honor because of the suffering of death' (= the resurrection and exaltation of Psalms 8:5b). Psalms 8:4 is the question the incarnation answers.
5: A direct connection: Hebrews 2:7,9 applies both halves of this verse to Christ. 'You made him for a little while lower than the angels' = the incarnation, in which the Son who is 'far superior to angels' (Hebrews 1:4) nevertheless 'was made lower than the angels' through taking on flesh and mortality. 'You have crowned him with glory and honor' = the resurrection and ascension: 'we see him who for a little while was made lower than the angels, namely Jesus, crowned with glory and honor because of the suffering of death' (Hebrews 2:9).
6: A direct connection: Paul quotes 'you have put all things under his feet' in 1 Corinthians 15:27 and the same language appears in Ephesians 1:22 ('he put all things under his feet and gave him as head over all things to the church') and Hebrews 2:8 ('leaving nothing outside his control'). The subjection of 'all things' under the son of man is fully realized in Christ's resurrection and reign. 1 Corinthians 15:24-28 clarifies: the subjection is not yet fully visible — Christ reigns now and will reign until all enemies including death itself are put under his feet.
7: A theme: 'all sheep and oxen, and also the beasts of the field' — the animals under human (and thus Christ's) dominion. The dominion mandate of Genesis 1:28 ('have dominion over the fish of the sea and over the birds of the heavens and over every living thing') is what Ps 8 celebrates and what Christ's resurrection-kingship restores. The groaning creation (Romans 8:19-22) awaits the full revelation of the children of God — when the dominion of Ps 8 is restored through the one who became the son of man to reclaim it.
8: A theme: 'the birds of the heavens, and the fish of the sea, whatever passes along the paths of the seas.' The completeness of the creation-dominion — birds, fish, sea-paths. Christ's mastery over creation anticipates the restored dominion: he commands fish (Luke 5:4-7; John 21:6), calms seas (Mark 4:39), walks on water (Matthew 14:25). These are not magic tricks but demonstrations that the son of man of Ps 8 has come — the one in whom the creation-dominion of Adam is restored and surpassed.
9: A direct connection: 'O LORD, our Lord, how majestic is your name in all the earth!' The psalm closes as it opened — with the majestic name. In the NT's reading of Ps 8, this final doxology is also the first: the name that is majestic 'in all the earth' is the name of Jesus, before whom 'every knee should bow, in heaven and on earth and under the earth, and every tongue confess that Jesus Christ is Lord, to the glory of God the Father' (Philippians 2:10-11). The earthly majestic name of Ps 8 becomes the cosmic-universal name of the crucified and risen Son.
